Git init Shows a master Branch Hint? Here's How to Set main as Default
Git init shows a master branch hint? Here's how to set main as default

Some of my students recently ran into a hint that says "master" is set as the initial branch name when they used Git, and they were curious about what was going on. So I wrote a quick post to log this down.
Why master got renamed to main
If you installed Git a long time ago and later updated to a newer version, you might see this message pop up when you run
language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

hint: Using 'master' as the name for the initial branch. This default branch name
hint: is subject to change. To configure the initial branch name to use in all
hint: of your new repositories, which will suppress this warning, call:
hint:
hint:   git config --global init.defaultBranch <name>
hint:
hint: Names commonly chosen instead of 'master' are 'main', 'trunk' and
hint: 'development'. The just-created branch can be renamed via this command:
hint:
hint:   git branch -m <name>
Initialized empty Git repository in /Users/UserName/GitHub/DailyTools/.git/
If you push to GitHub, you'll also see a prompt suggesting you rename the default branch to main.

So why did this happen? It traces back to 2020 — the killing of George Floyd. The short version: a Black American man was killed by excessive police force, which triggered global protests and pushed a lot of industries to re-examine racial bias and inequality.

The US tech industry started looking at common naming conventions that might carry racist undertones — words like "master", "slave", "blacklist", "whitelist" — terms that can evoke the history of slavery and discrimination.
So in 2020, GitHub announced they were changing the default branch name from "master" to "main" to push toward more inclusive naming. Other tech companies and open-source communities followed suit.
The "blacklist" / "whitelist" pair you see all over the place has also been proposed to switch to "blocklist" / "allowlist".
How to change the default branch name
So how do we actually shut this hint up? Easy — run this command:
This tells your global Git config to use main as the default initial branch. From now on,
If you've got an existing repo that's already running, you can rename its current branch like this:

git switch master # Switch to the master branch
git branch -m main # Rename master to main
git push -u origin main # Push main to the remote and set it as upstream
# Push main to the remote and set it as upstream
Then head over to GitHub → Settings → Branches and change the default branch to main. Once that's done, delete the remote master branch:
git push origin --delete master # Delete the remote master branch
If you have Branch Protection Rules turned on, you need to delete the protection rule on master first, then set up a new one on main. Otherwise you won't be able to delete the remote master branch.
For team projects, after you've done all this, the rest of the team needs to run these commands to update their local tracking branches:

git fetch origin # Fetch the latest remote state
git branch -u origin/main main # Make local main track remote main
git remote set-head origin -a # Update which branch the remote HEAD points to
# Make local main track remote main
# Update which branch the remote HEAD points to
And that's it — master is now main.
